ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'fried oyster' class; no specific Japanese kaki fry class exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers cooked oysters broadly; no specific breaded deep-fried oyster code exists. Closest parent match used; breadcrumb coating and deep-frying preparation noted here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for kaki fry or breaded fried oyster found in published SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.description: 4 pieces of fried oysters with sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of kaki fry is 4 pieces of breaded deep-fried oysters with accompanying sauce, weighing approximately 180 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Oysters, Pacific, cooked, breaded and fried (FDC ID 175168)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 175168 (Oysters, Pacific, cooked, breaded and fried) used as the primary nutrient anchor. This is an approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Input record values (provenance: GrabFood ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for this preparation and have been retained where consistent. Monounsaturated fat (6.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (3.9 g/100 g), and potassium (210 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168. The input source listing foodo.sg was a menu-listing reference only and is not used as a nutrient evidence source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kaki fry (Japanese breaded deep-fried oysters). GI estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy with breaded and fried fish and seafood dishes with panko breadcrumb coating, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried fish with breadcrumb coating (GI approximately 65). The panko breadcrumb coating is the dominant carbohydrate source and drives GI. GL per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 x 25.0) / 100 = 16.2, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 13.89 x 1.8 = 25.0 g. University of Sydney GI Database is an approved GI-specific source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 333 mg per 100 g; a 180 g serving delivers approximately 600 mg sodium, representing 26% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, compounded by an unknown-sodium special sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kaki fry to a small portion (2 pieces), request the sauce on the side or omit it entirely, and avoid adding soy sauce to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Panko breadcrumb coating contributes 13.9 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.56 g dietary fibre, yielding a medium GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of 16.2 per 180 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 2 pieces, pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response, and check the special sauce for added sugar before consuming.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 16.7 g per 100 g with saturated fat 4.44 g and trans fat 0.28 g per 100 g; a 180 g serving delivers approximately 30 g total fat and 0.5 g trans fat, which is clinically significant for cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit kaki fry to an occasional small portion, choose steamed or grilled oyster preparations as a lower-fat alternative, and avoid adding high-sodium condiments that further elevate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; the dish lacks meaningful omega-3 anti-inflammatory components in the final prepared form.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried oyster preparations and instead choose steamed or grilled oysters, which preserve the anti-inflammatory zinc and iron content without generating pro-inflammatory AGEs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kaki Fry (カキフライ, Kaki Furai) is a classic Japanese yoshoku dish consisting of Pacific oysters coated in wheat flour, beaten egg, and panko breadcrumbs, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp. The dish is typically served with tonkatsu sauce, tartar sauce, or a restaurant-specific special sauce, along with shredded cabbage and lemon. In Singapore's Japanese restaurant and food-delivery context, it is commonly offered as a set of 4 pieces. The exterior is crunchy and golden due to the panko coating, while the interior oyster is tender, briny, and creamy. Nutritionally, a 180 g serving (4 pieces) provides approximately 450 kcal, 20 g protein, 30 g fat, and 25 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 600 mg per serving, primarily from the sauce and the natural salinity of oysters. The dish is a moderate glycemic load food (GL approximately 16) due to the refined panko coating. Oysters are a natural source of iron (2.2 mg per 100 g), zinc, and vitamin B12, but the deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that reduce the net anti-inflammatory benefit. Kaki fry is not suitable for individuals with shellfish or gluten allergies. It is best consumed occasionally and in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kaki fry?
Is kaki fry suitable for diabetics?
カキフライ カロリー
Is kaki fry high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values from the input record (GrabFood, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for Pacific oysters breaded and fried (FDC ID 175168). No exact Singapore restaurant-specific lab analysis is available. USDA adapted match used to validate and anchor values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for breaded fried seafood. Confidence set to medium.
notes: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture breakdown for this specific restaurant preparation of kaki fry is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175168) or Singapore HPB database at the level of a fully prepared dish with sauce as of 2026. USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168 (Oysters, Pacific, cooked, breaded and fried) was consulted and used for macro validation; full micronutrient panel for the composite dish including sauce is not reported at this level of specificity. Monounsaturated fat (6.8 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (3.9 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168 for breaded fried Pacific oysters. Potassium (210 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168. No zero-value core macros are present in this record. Input ethnic_tags included 'Alcoholic Drink' and 'Japanese Rice Bowl' which are clearly erroneous for this dish and have been removed. Country of origin in input was listed as Singapore; corrected to Japan as the dish's culinary origin, with Singapore as the serving context. The input source listing foodo.sg was a menu-listing reference only and is not used as a nutrient evidence source; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the approved nutrient anchor.
